content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : John Corzine                                    Date :May 18, 1979
    Office of the Legislative Counsel
    925 L Street, Suite 500
    Sacramento, CA  95814
                                                     M1-79-139

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Sarah Cameron

Subject: Reportability of loans to a business entity


       This is confirmation of our recent telephone conversations
on the above-noted subject.  The staff of the Fair Political
Practices Commission has consistently interpreted the term
"gross receipts" as used in Government Code Section 87207(b)(3)
as not including loans received by the business entity whose
income is being reported under its provisions. This limit is
on disclosure only, however, and loans to business entities
are not excluded from the term "income" as used throughout the
Political Reform Act.  Consequently, loans which do not have to
be reported may nevertheless on occasion disqualify under the
provisions of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. Although
these interpretations have not been the subject of a formal
opinion by the Commission, they were acknowledged and confirmed
by the Commission during its discussions of proposed legislation
to make outstanding loans reportable.  These discussions were
held at the Commission's March and April meetings this year.

       If any of your designated employees has a 10% or greater
interest in a bank, you may also be interested in knowing that
"gross receipts" does not include repayment to the bank of the
principal of loans but it does include the payment of interest.

STC:nrw

sections.question_synthetic: Whether loans received by a business entity are reportable as 'gross receipts' under Government Code Section 87207(b)(3), and how loans affect disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC interprets 'gross receipts' as excluding loans received by business entities for disclosure purposes; however, loans are not excluded from 'income' generally and may trigger disqualification under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. Interest payments on loans are included in gross receipts, but principal repayments are not.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Memorandum format without formal Q/A sections; conclusion synthesized from body text discussing FPPC's consistent interpretation; document confirms telephone conversation discussion and references Commission meetings rather than formal opinion.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether loans received by a business entity are reportable as 'gross receipts' under Government Code Section 87207(b)(3), and how loans affect disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC interprets 'gross receipts' as excluding loans received by business entities for disclosure purposes; however, loans are not excluded from 'income' generally and may trigger disqualification under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. Interest payments on loans are included in gross receipts, but principal repayments are not.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : John Corzine Date :May 18, 1979 Office of the Legislative Counsel 925 L Street, Suite 500 Sacramento, CA 95814 M1-79-139 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Sarah Cameron Subject: Reportability of loans to a business entity This is confirmation of our recent telephone conversations on the above-noted subject. The staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission has consistently interpreted the term "gross receipts" as used in Government Code Section 87207(b)(3) as not including loans received by the business entity whose income is being reported under its provisions. This limit is on disclosure only, however, and loans to business entities are not excluded from the term "income" as used throughout the Political Reform Act. Consequently, loans which do not have to be reported may nevertheless on occasion disqualify under the provisions of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. Although these interpretations have not been the subject of a formal opinion by the Commission, they were acknowledged and confirmed by the Commission during its discussions of proposed legislation to make outstanding loans reportable. These discussions were held at the Commission's March and April meetings this year. If any of your designated employees has a 10% or greater interest in a bank, you may also be interested in knowing that "gross receipts" does not include repayment to the bank of the principal of loans but it does include the payment of interest. STC:nrw
embedding.summary: FPPC confirms its longstanding interpretation that loans to business entities are not reportable as 'gross receipts' for disclosure purposes, but may still trigger disqualification obligations under the Political Reform Act.
